content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 1 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_001_page_001.jpg

---

**1:1** Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the church of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ:

**1:2** Grace to you and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

**1:3** We are bound to give thanks to God always for you, brothers, as is fitting, because your faith grows exceedingly, and the love of each one of you all toward one another abounds,

**1:4** so that we ourselves boast of you among the churches of God, for your endurance and faith in all your persecutions and the afflictions that you are bearing --

**1:5** a clear evidence of the righteous judgment of God, that you may be counted worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you also suffer;

**1:6** since indeed it is righteous before God to repay with affliction those who afflict you,

**1:7** and to grant rest to you who are afflicted, along with us, at the revelation of the Lord Jesus from heaven with the angels of his power,

**1:8** in fire of flame, bringing vengeance on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus,

**1:9** who will pay the penalty of eternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his might,

**1:10** when he comes to be glorified among his saints and to be marveled at among all who believed -- because our testimony to you was believed -- in that day.

**1:11** To this end we also pray always for you, that our God may count you worthy of the calling, and may fulfill every good desire and every work of faith with power,

**1:12** so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.

---

## Council Notes

**Manuscript Observations:** The source folio (Codex Sinaiticus, page 204) displays 2 Thessalonians beginning in the first column, running in scriptio continua across four columns. The text is well-preserved and legible. Nomina sacra are employed throughout for God (THU/THW), Lord (KU/KW), Jesus (IU), and Christ (CHU). The chapter covers approximately the first two columns, with verse 8 beginning near the column boundary. The transition from 1 Thessalonians is unmarked by any major decoration.

**Key Consensus Decisions:**

1. **"Church" vs. "assembly" (v.1):** EMET renders EKKLHSIA as "assembly" with a bracket gloss; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "church." The consensus adopts "church" as the standard English rendering that is both accurate and immediately comprehensible. The word denotes a gathered community, and "church" has become the standard translation convention across traditions.

2. **"We are bound" vs. "we ought" vs. "we owe it" (v.3):** OPHEILOMEN carries the sense of moral obligation. EMET's "we ought" is accurate but slightly weak; PNEUMA's "we owe it to God" is vivid but paraphrastic. LOGOS's "we are bound" captures the obligatory force while remaining formal. Consensus follows LOGOS.

3. **"Grows exceedingly" vs. "flourishing" (v.3):** HYPERAUXANEI is a NT hapax with the intensifying prefix HYPER-. EMET and LOGOS both use "grows exceedingly," which preserves the compound structure. PNEUMA's "flourishing" is elegant but loses the intensifying prefix. Consensus retains "grows exceedingly."

4. **"Brothers" vs. "brothers and sisters" (v.3):** PNEUMA adds "and sisters" to ADELPHOI. While the inclusive sense is likely intended by Paul, the Sinaiticus text reads only ADELPHOI. Consensus retains "brothers" as the manuscript reading, acknowledging the inclusive reference in spirit.

5. **"Endurance" vs. "steadfastness" (v.4):** HYPOMONHS can be rendered either way. EMET uses "endurance," LOGOS uses "steadfastness," PNEUMA uses "endurance." Consensus adopts "endurance" by majority agreement.

6. **"Clear evidence" vs. "manifest token" (v.5):** ENDEIGMA (hapax) demands a rendering that conveys forensic proof. EMET's "manifest token" is archaic; PNEUMA's "evidence" is clear; LOGOS's "clear evidence" balances precision and readability. Consensus follows LOGOS.

7. **"In fire of flame" vs. "in flaming fire" vs. "in blazing fire" (v.8):** The Sinaiticus reads EN PYRI PHLOGOS ("in fire of flame"), not the reversed variant. EMET and LOGOS render "in flaming fire," which adjusts the word order; PNEUMA uses "blazing fire," adding interpretive intensity. Consensus renders "in fire of flame" to preserve the Sinaiticus genitive construction faithfully.

8. **"Gospel" vs. "good news" (v.8):** EUANGELION is rendered "good news" by EMET and PNEUMA, and "gospel" by LOGOS. "Gospel" is the traditional and widely recognized rendering; "good news" is a literal calque. Consensus adopts "gospel" as the more established English term.

9. **"Away from the presence" vs. "from the face" vs. "shut out from" (v.9):** APO PROSOPOU TOU KURIOU literally means "from the face of the Lord." EMET preserves "from the face"; LOGOS uses "away from the presence"; PNEUMA interprets as "shut out from the presence." Consensus uses "away from the presence" as a balance -- it clarifies the spatial metaphor without adding the interpretive "shut out" language absent from the Greek.

10. **"Might" vs. "strength" vs. "power" (v.9):** ISCHYOS AUTOU is rendered "his strength" (EMET), "his might" (LOGOS), or "his power" (PNEUMA). PNEUMA's "power" overlaps with DYNAMIS elsewhere. Consensus uses "his might" (LOGOS) to distinguish ISCHYS from DYNAMIS.

11. **Treatment of the parenthetical (v.10):** All three translators handle the awkward parenthetical clause differently. EMET preserves it inline; LOGOS uses em dashes; PNEUMA splits it into a separate sentence. Consensus uses em dashes (following LOGOS) to mark the aside while keeping the verse as a single syntactic unit, which reflects the Greek.

12. **"Good desire" vs. "good pleasure of goodness" (v.11):** EUDOKIAN AGATHOSYNES is a dense genitive. EMET gives "good pleasure of goodness"; LOGOS gives "every desire of goodness"; PNEUMA restructures as "every good purpose." Consensus renders "every good desire," compressing the genitive into an adjective-noun pair that preserves both the volitional sense (EUDOKIA) and the moral quality (AGATHOSYNE).

13. **"To this end" vs. "for which" vs. "with this in mind" (v.11):** The transitional phrase EIS HO KAI is rendered variously. EMET's "for which" is terse; PNEUMA's "with this in mind" is paraphrastic; LOGOS's "to this end" is precise and formal. Consensus follows LOGOS.

14. **"With power" vs. "in power" vs. "by his power" (v.11):** EN DYNAMEI is rendered "in power" (EMET), "with power" (LOGOS), or "by his power" (PNEUMA). PNEUMA adds the possessive "his" which is not in the Greek. Consensus uses "with power" (LOGOS) as an accurate instrumental rendering.

--- SOPHIA, Arbiter of the Council
